page: Amazon Web Services c6id.2xlarge is a compute-optimized instance powered by an Intel Xeon 8375C processor running at 3.5 GHz. Built on the x86_64 architecture, this instance provides 8 vCPUs across 4 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. It features 16.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz, resulting in a 2.0 GB per core memory allocation. The instance is equipped with 474 GB of local NVMe SSD instance storage and operates on the AWS Nitro hypervisor with a baseline network bandwidth of 3 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates average performance across most standard compute and memory operations. The instance achieved a Geekbench single-core score of 1610.0 and a multi-core score of 6004.0. In memory bandwidth tests, it recorded 413.67 GB/sec for cached small-block reads and 58.2 GB/sec for uncached large-block reads. Cryptographic and vector processing capabilities are supported by hardware flags such as AVX-512 and AES, yielding a Passmark CPU encryption score of 7267.11. However, multi-threaded bzip3 decompression and medium-sized LLM inference tasks show weaker performance, with text generation for a 7B model reaching 8.21 tokens/sec.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the c6id.2xlarge balances dedicated compute resources with high-speed local NVMe storage, making it a viable option for workloads requiring fast scratch space without the overhead of network-attached storage. The 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio limits its suitability for memory-intensive databases, but it is well-suited for compute-bound tasks such as static web serving, software compilation, and moderate database operations, as demonstrated by its 12.34 GB/sec static web serving throughput and 7.96 million operations/second in Redis SET benchmarks.
description: Amazon Web Services c6id.2xlarge is an x86_64 compute-optimized instance featuring 8 vCPUs, 4 physical cores, and 16.0 GB of DDR4 memory. Powered by an Intel Xeon 8375C processor running at 3.5 GHz, it includes 474 GB of local NVMe SSD storage and runs on the Nitro hypervisor with a 3 Gbps baseline network. Performance benchmarks show average results, including a Geekbench multi-core score of 6004.0 and a Redis SET throughput of 7.96 million operations per second. While its 1:2 memory-to-vCPU ratio and weaker multi-threaded decompression performance present tradeoffs for memory-heavy tasks, its local NVMe storage provides qualitative cost efficiency for applications needing fast local read/write speeds. It is suited for static web serving, software compilation, and database operations.
